ev_159.tag: Nuclear capability deters attack through existential threat
ev_159.cite: Early 18 , Chair of the Department of Public Administration, Professor in the Department of Political Science at the University of Albany, 10-2-2018, “Nuclear Weapons, Existential Threats, and The Stability–Instability Paradox” The Nonproliferation Review, Accessed via Taylor & Francis, Access Provided by Minnesota Libraries
ev_159.fullcite: Early 18 , Chair of the Department of Public Administration, Professor in the Department of Political Science at the University of Albany, 10-2-2018, “Nuclear Weapons, Existential Threats, and The Stability–Instability Paradox” The Nonproliferation Review, Accessed via Taylor & Francis, Access Provided by Minnesota Libraries
ev_159.full_text: Recent scholarship has largely ignored systematic differences in the existential threats that nuclear-weapon possessors pose to other states. This study theorizes that the capacity to pose existential threats shapes nuclear-armed states’ willingness to use military force against one another. We explore three hypotheses regarding how nuclear-based existential threats can deter conflict or encourage it, including under the conditions proposed by the stability–instability paradox. We rely on a statistical analysis of nuclear-armed dyads from 1950 to 2001 and employ the Nuclear Annihilation Threat (NAT) Index to capture variation in the existential threats nuclear-armed states pose to one another. We find that being able to pose an existential threat to another state emboldens potential initiators to use military force but does not deter attacks. The emboldening effects are particularly strong under the hypothesized conditions of the stability–instability paradox. Our study provides unique contributions to ongoing debates over the political effects of nuclear weapons. Writing in 1946, nuclear strategist Bernard Brodie hypothesized that nuclear weapons differed from conventional military force in that “superiority in numbers of bombs is not itself a guarantee of strategic superiority in atomic bomb warfare.” 1 As he went on to discuss, the fact that nuclear weapons allow states to annihilate one another differentiates them from past types of weapons—creating a fundamentally different type of security relationship between possessor states than exists in the conventional realm. Yet after this initial argument was made, others—including Brodie himself—questioned whether nuclear superiority might matter in similar ways as conventional superiority in affecting states’ foreign-policy behavior.2 While scholars have been actively searching for generalizable evidence to resolve the nuclear-superiority debate,3 far less attention has been given to evaluating the argument that nuclear weapons are best understood in terms of the specific threats they pose to states’ survival. According to this perspective, the impact of strategic nuclear attacks on a state’s survival can range from significant but survivable consequences to complete annihilation. Since a great deal of variation exists in the degree to which nuclear-armed states pose existential threats to one another, there is strong reason to think that nuclear-armed states will behave differently toward one another based on the severity of those threats.4 In 1955, for example, China’s Mao Zedong famously rejected the notion that it was vulnerable to US nuclear threats. He stated that “The Chinese people are not to be cowed by U.S. atomic blackmail. Our country has a population of 600 million and an area of 9,600,000 square kilometres. The United States cannot annihilate the Chinese nation with its small stack of atom bombs.” 5 In contrast, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has repeatedly asserted that Iran’s acquisition of even a small nuclear arsenal represents an existential threat to Israel.6 As these quotations indicate, leaders contextualize foreign nuclear threats in relation to their own states’ circumstances. In this study, we use the concept of existential threats to reframe existing debates over the impact of nuclear weapons on international conflict7 and explore its implications for those theories. We define “existential threats” as those capable of jeopardizing the continued political and economic viability of nation-states. While other types of existential threats exist,8 nuclear-based existential threats are unique in that “they have the potential ability to quickly and completely annihilate their targets’ militaries, populations, and infrastructure.” 9 During his speech to the United Nations on September 19, 2017, for example, US President Donald Trump explicitly framed his deterrent threat to North Korea in existential terms. He stated that “The United States has great strength and patience, but if it is forced to defend itself or its allies, we will have no choice but to totally destroy North Korea.” Given the escalating threats previously exchanged between President Trump and North Korea’s Kim Jong Un, this threat was meant to signal US resolve to employ its nuclear arsenal to annihilate North Korea if necessary to protect US security interests. Such threats are a commonly understood component of nuclear deterrence, though rarely are they so explicitly invoked. An existential perspective on nuclear threats focuses on the amount of destruction that a nuclear-armed state can credibly threaten to inflict upon a target state. The concept of existential threats is central to understanding the theoretical foundations for phenomena such as the stability–instability paradox, and also plays a pervasive—if often undertheorized—role in most other accounts of nuclear security.10 We argue that the severity of nuclear-based existential threats varies on the basis of states’ arsenal sizes, delivery capabilities, geographic and political relationship with the target, and the target’s demographic vulnerability to such an attack. According to this perspective, effectively capturing how states perceive nuclear-based existential threats requires more than just looking at the presence or size of nuclear-weapons arsenals. Based on existing theories, we develop and test three separate logics for how nuclear based existential threats could: (1) deter military aggression; (2) embolden military aggression; or, (3) encourage aggression under the more limited conditions predicted by the stability–instability paradox. Our deterrence hypothesis predicts that a state that is capable of posing salient existential threats against other nuclear-armed states discourages those states from attacking it. Our emboldening hypothesis predicts that being able to pose more salient existential threats to nuclear-armed targets encourages higher levels of conventional military aggression. Lastly, we use the logic of the stability–instability paradox to hypothesize that states are most likely to engage in aggressive military behavior in nuclear armed dyads when both states pose salient existential threats to one another. We test our hypotheses via a statistical analysis of the conflict behavior of nuclear armed states from 1950 to 2001.11 We focus our analysis on pairs of nuclear-armed states in order to contribute to the ongoing debate over the role nuclear-weapons capabilities play in shaping nuclear-armed states’ relationships with one another.12 To operationalize our concept of existential threats, we rely on the Nuclear Annihilation Threat (NAT) Index, which measures the specific existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to other states.13 The NAT Index captures how nuclear arsenal size, vulnerability of urban populations, ballistic-missile arsenals, rivalry, and political instability shape the salience of existential threats. Using this measure allows us to evaluate how differences in the existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to one another influence their willingness to engage in militarized aggression. We employ the initiation of militarized interstate disputes (MIDs) as our dependent variable,14 which captures a broad spectrum of cases in which states engage in militarized aggression. Our statistical analysis finds that the presence of more salient existential threats among nuclear-armed states encourages rather than deters militarized aggression. Our findings reveal almost no support for the deterrence-based hypothesis. Instead, we find that being capable of posing more salient existential threats to fellow nuclear armed states is associated with significantly higher probabilities of military aggression. Consistent with the stability–instability paradox, we find that the probability that a nuclear-armed state will engage in militarized aggression against a fellow nuclear state is highest when both pose salient existential threats to one another. Rather than discouraging the use of violence, the presence of salient existential threats in cases like the United States and Soviet Union, the United States and China, and India and Pakistan appears to embolden those states to initiate militarized disputes with one other. None of the episodes in our study escalated into a major war, but that is always a risk once conflicts begin. To date, however, the broader predictions of the stability–instability paradox have held. Our study offers important new insights into how nuclear weapons impact international relations. Employing the NAT Index allowed our analysis to capture the more nuanced predictions of the stability–instability paradox in ways that previous quantitative analyses could not.15 In testing the theory using measures that more accurately reflected its arguments, we found strong support for its predictions. Substantively, our findings suggest that neither nuclear optimists nor nuclear pessimists are exactly right.16 While deterrence appears to have prevented a nuclear war from ever being fought between nuclear-armed states, the states that pose the most extreme existential threats to one another have been the most prone to initiate minor-level conflicts against one another. This suggests that higher levels of conventional conflict will be associated with increasing amounts of vertical proliferation amongst nuclear states which make them more dangerous to each other, especially amongst rivals. Our findings also contribute to the ongoing debate between “nuclear coercionists” and “nuclear skeptics.” 17 We advance this debate by reconceiving the debate’s conceptual foundations of how states perceive nuclear-based threats. While our findings do not speak to whether nuclear coercion is an effective tactic, they do uncover the circumstances under which nuclear-armed states are most likely to use military aggression to try to obtain what they want from each other. Finally, our study suggests that using more complex measures to capture nuclear-security threats can help advance the debates over the role nuclear weapons play in shaping international relations. We acknowledge that the NAT Index is not a perfect proxy for the ways that policy makers view nuclear-security threats—and there may be ways of improving it. Yet, the approach provides far more nuance than measures of nuclear-arsenal size in capturing how we think policy makers perceive nuclear threats. Our analysis indicates that the NAT Index appears to effectively capture how nuclear-armed states evaluate and act upon the dangers they pose to one another, especially when compared with more simplistic capabilities-based measures. Our findings suggest that more sophisticated empirical measures for nuclear threats could be the key to advancing theoretical debates over nuclear security. Explanations of how nuclear weapons affect international coercion and conflict Nuclear weapons represent a fundamentally different type of security threat than conventional weapons.18 They are different from other weapons because of their destructive scope and the immediacy with which such destruction can be inflicted. As nuclear strategist Thomas Schelling highlights, the ability to strike an opponent with nuclear weapons using delivery vehicles like ballistic missiles provides an unprecedented capacity to annihilate a state in what was, previously, an unfathomably short amount of time. Furthermore, nuclear weapons enable their possessors to threaten or destroy an adversary’s population, infrastructure, and valued possessions without having to defeat them on the field of battle first.19 As such, nuclear weapons separate the ability to inflict damage and/or pain from the need to win or even engage in conventional military conflicts. This led scholars to begin distinguishing between conventional distributions of power versus the distribution of nuclear-weapon-based power.20 Various theories have emerged about how the possession of nuclear weapons influences states’ national-security strategies and conflict behavior, especially among dyads that both possess nuclear weapons. One perspective argues that nuclear weapons impose restraints upon how nuclear powers deal with one another. Scholars, such as Schelling, have argued that nuclear weapons are the ultimate weapons of deterrence.21 Nuclear weapons enable their possessors to threaten states with unacceptable costs if they engage in the behaviors their possessors seek to deter. According to Columbia University’s Robert Jervis, “Deterrence comes from having enough weapons to destroy the other’s cities; this capability is an absolute, not a relative, one.” 22 This suggests that the “balance of terror” created by the distribution of nuclear-weapon-related power operates differently than the balance of power created by the distribution of conventional military power.23 Possessing one’s own nuclear weapons can help ensure that nuclear weapons are not used against one’s state and can deter other forms of conflict as well. Two of the strongest empirical observations that support this argument are that nuclear-weapons possessors have rarely fought major conflicts with one another24 and that nuclear-weapons possessors have never used such weapons against one another. Jervis argues that the effects of nuclear weapons also extend to nuclear-weapons possessors’ willingness to engage in low-level conflicts with one another. He argues that, “because force cannot be easily controlled or compartmentalized, the fear of nuclear war does deter the other side from much more than nuclear attack.” 25 International-relations theorist Kenneth Waltz similarly argues that nuclear weapons enhance international stability by making the potential costs associated with attacking nuclear-weapons possessors too great for states whose primary goal is survival.26 These perspectives all share a common argument that nuclear weapons enhance their possessors’ ability to deter foreign military attacks, even if variation exists in how far those deterrent effects extend to low levels of conflict and/or threats of military aggression. Another perspective argues that possessing a larger, more threatening nuclear arsenal than a nuclear-armed counterpart provides potential military and coercive advantages.27 According to this perspective, possessing nuclear superiority over another nuclear-armed state enables and/or encourages leaders to be more aggressive in their dealings with weaker nuclear powers. Proponents of this view further argue that the nuclear threats states pose to one another are not absolute or monolithic; rather, they are subject to variance and can be evaluated relationally within dyads.28 They argue that the distribution of nuclearweapon-based power can best be evaluated similarly to the distribution of conventional power, which can be balanced or allow one side to have a relative advantage. Possessing nuclear superiority, according to some scholars, can enable states to be more effective in coercing their counterparts or in obtaining their objectives within international crises.29 As a potential cognitive origin for this vein of thinking, Jervis notes that, even if possessing nuclear superiority does not provide states with a real military advantage, it could still embolden leaders to behave more aggressively if they think it does.30 More recently, Georgetown’s Matthew Kroenig has argued that possessing nuclear superiority emboldens leaders to be more resolved in international crises—advantaging them in brinksmanship competitions and allowing them to succeed more often.31 This argument builds on past works by game theorists which argue that nuclear bargaining can be conceived as a competition in risk taking in which whichever party flinches first loses.32 Offering a competing argument, however, University of Virginia’s Todd Sechser and Texas A&M’s Matthew Fuhrmann argue that nuclear superiority and nuclear weapons more generally offer states little advantage in seeking to coerce other states.33 Whereas Kroenig found evidence that nuclear superiority was positively correlated with states achieving their goals within international crises, Sechser and Fuhrmann find no evidence that nuclear weapons or nuclear superiority contributed to the effectiveness of states’ coercive efforts.34 The stability–instability paradox adopts elements from both perspectives. This theory argues that, when the leaders of two nuclear-armed states view the consequences of a full-scale nuclear war as being unthinkably costly, they will prevent their relationships from ever deteriorating into such a war. This conceptualization of the stability–instability paradox has been referred to as the “red-line” model, in which nuclear-armed adversaries “are free to pursue sub-strategic political objectives through militarized conflict without fear that the terms of such conflict will escalate beyond their control and jeopardize their strategic interests.” 35 Counterintuitively, it is the mutual recognition by both disputants that a full-blown nuclear war would be calamitous that can embolden leaders to initiate minor conflicts due to the expectation that neither side has an incentive to let conflicts get out of hand.36 Contrary to Jervis’s argument that leaders avoid exacerbating the risk of nuclear annihilation,37 this suggests that leaders are willing to accept at least some risk that their aggressive behavior could spiral into an unwanted nuclear war out of confidence that expectations of mutually assured destruction will prevent escalation. Despite a dearth of supporting evidence, the stability–instability paradox remains prevalent within policy circles and has continued to be taken seriously by scholars. Jervis rejects its claims and concludes that nuclear weapons make “even bold and foolish decision-makers behave cautiously,” encouraging them to “shy away from adventures that will lead to disaster.” 38 Researchers have arrived at conflicting conclusions with respect to the stability–instability paradox even when looking at the same case. In his study of the India–Pakistan nuclear relationship, for example, the Naval Postgraduate School’s S. Paul Kapur finds no evidence that the stability–instability paradox can explain the two states’ track record of conflicts after they acquired nuclear weapons.39 In contrast, the Australian Catholic University’s Christopher Watterson finds supportive evidence for the red-line model of the stability–instability paradox in examining the Kargil conflict.40 A more recent analysis contradicts the expectations of the stability– instability paradox, finding that “dyads in which both states possess nuclear weapons are not significantly less likely to fight wars, nor are they significantly more or less belligerent at low levels of conflict.” 41 A growing number of quantitative studies have found that possessing nuclear weapons does not have a salient impact on states’ likelihood of engaging in aggressive behaviors. Some research has shown, for example, that nuclear-armed states have no stronger propensity to initiate disputes than states that do not possess nuclear weapons.42 Using a more nuanced measure that captures whether nuclear-armed states can strike each other with their nuclear weapons, the University of California—San Diego’s Erik Gartzke and his coauthors similarly find no evidence that possessing such capabilities influences the likelihood of conflict between nuclear-armed states.43 Sechser and Fuhrmann find that nuclear weapons do not positively affect their possessors’ outcomes in territorial disputes or encourage possessors to employ military force within them.44 The University of Pennsylvania’s Michael Horowitz finds that states that have recently acquired nuclear weapons are more likely to become involved in militarized disputes, but this effect fades the longer they possess them.45 With respect to the deterrent value of nuclear weapons, Louisiana State University’s David Sobek and his coauthors find that nuclear-armed states are no less at risk of being attacked than non-nuclear states.46 Looking instead at the effects of having a nuclear ally, Fuhrman and Sechser find evidence that possessing nuclear-armed allies actually does deter militarized conflict but hosting foreign deployed nuclear weapons does not.47 Adopting an innovative approach, Massachusetts Institute of Technology’s Vipin Narang developed a conceptual measure to capture states’ nuclear postures. Using this approach, Narang finds no evidence “that mere possession of nuclear weapons—or even secure second-strike forces—systematically deters conventional conflict.” 48 Taken as a whole, the literature on the impact that nuclear weapons have on state behaviors yields a number of well-defined perspectives but lacks a consensus on which perspectives are most strongly supported by the available evidence. This has been particularly true for the recent findings from large-n studies, which have produced a myriad of contradictory results. We argue that one of the reasons for these mixed findings is that most quantitative studies have employed crude measures of the nuclear threats that states pose to one another, such as dichotomous possession variables or counts of nuclear-arsenal size. These measures are problematic because they are unable to capture many of the important differences that shape the nature of nuclear threats. For example, South Africa only possessed a handful of nuclear weapons and had no effective capabilities to deliver them against other nuclear powers during the 1980s. In contrast, the Soviet Union had thousands of strategic nuclear weapons and possessed numerous delivery platforms, like ballistic missiles, that provided its nuclear forces with global and rapid reach. It is highly problematic to treat the nuclear threats South Africa and the Soviet Union posed to each other as equivalent, yet that is what quantitative studies that rely on dichotomous measures of nuclear weapons possession have done. Substantial improvements can thus be made in how the quantitatively oriented nuclear-security literature approaches the task of capturing nuclear weapons’ effects on state behavior. Explaining the effects of nuclear-based existential threats on military aggression The defining attribute of nuclear weapons that differentiates them from conventional weapons is their ability to pose existential threats that can be executed with shocking rapidity.49 Nuclear weapons can utterly destroy a nation’s military, population, and infrastructure and there are no effective, large-scale defenses against such threats. In framing the role played by nuclear weapons in shaping states’ security strategies, numerous nuclear theorists and policy makers discuss their impact in terms of the unique existential threats they pose.50 At the same time, however, much of the theorizing that undergirds arguments about the existential threats posed by nuclear-armed states was derived from analyzing the US–Soviet dyad during the Cold War.51 As other states have proliferated and acquired much more limited nuclear capabilities, capturing variation in the nature of nuclear-based threats has become increasingly important. Even more so than theories premised on the logic of minimal nuclear deterrence,52 theories premised on the existential dimension of nuclear-weapons threats must be able to capture variation in whether states are capable of posing truly existential threats or not. We explore how nuclear-based existential threats affect militarized interstate aggression between nuclear-armed states by developing and testing hypotheses from a number of different theoretical perspectives. While most MIDs have only resulted in minor disputes, potential conflict initiators can never be certain about the extent to which militarized disputes will escalate. Indeed, differing expectations about the perceived likelihoods of escalation across various theoretical perspectives give rise to different expectations about the effects of existential threats on whether states will initiate disputes. Existential threats touch upon the core goal that most major theories of international politics assume states prioritize above all else: survival.53 Leaders are inclined to engage in the aggressive use of military force when the expected benefits of initiating a conflict outweigh the prospective benefits of inaction—either immediately or down the line. For nuclear-armed states considering whether or not to undertake aggressive military action against one another, the risk of a dispute escalating to a point at which nuclear weapons become involved cannot be ignored. We argue that the salience of the existential threats leaders perceive can influence both the risks and consequences portions of their decision-making calculus in weighing whether to initiate a militarized dispute. This insight overlaps with the extent to which we assume that leaders discount or are sensitive to the threats and opportunities created by nuclear weapons, which vary according to the perspectives discussed above. The existential threats that nuclear-armed states project and the reciprocal vulnerabilities they face can shape whether leaders demonstrate greater confidence or caution in using military force against one another. Nuclear weapons and existential threats Just as not all conventional security threats are created equal, neither are all existential threats. Indeed, Narang critiques the existing nuclear-studies literature as having an “existential bias by assuming that the size, structure, and orientation of nuclear arsenals are all irrelevant.” 54 Indeed, simply possessing nuclear weapons does not automatically enable a state to pose existential threats to other states. While any nuclear war that takes place will entail extraordinary devastation, there are gradations to the possible extent of that devastation. Along those lines, nuclear strategist Herman Kahn asserts that the deaths of ten million versus 100 million as the result of a nuclear war are both tragic, but that the former is far more survivable for a state like the United States than the latter.55 Cross-national differences exist as well. Based upon the scope of the destruction inflicted by a nuclear war, some nations may be better equipped to survive it, depending upon the size and distribution of their populations (e.g., Russia in comparison with Israel). Precisely estimating how much of a state’s population, infrastructure, wealth, etc. must be destroyed to pose a salient existential threat is difficult to do. Yet, intuitively, we can imagine that a continuum exists along which the existential threat a nuclear-weapons possessor poses to another state grows from a minimal level to one that almost certainly assures destruction. We view three dimensions of existential threats as essential to capturing the variation in nuclear-armed states’ capacity to pose such threats to other states. The first component is the relational amount of nuclear destruction a nuclear-weapon possessor can inflict on a target state, dependent upon the size of its arsenal and the size and dispersion of the target’s population. In one of the seminal pieces that helped define the emergent study and practice of nuclear strategy, Brodie laid out that the primary utility of nuclear weapons is in threatening targets’ urban population centers.56 We view the extent to which a nuclear-weapon state can credibly threaten the annihilation of a target’s major urban centers as the single most important aspect that defines the existential threat they pose. The scale of the potential destruction a nuclear-armed state can inflict also matters in accounting for the salience of an existential threat. This salience thus depends upon how their nuclear capabilities translate into being able to jeopardize the varying populations of other states. North Korea’s small nuclear-weapon arsenal, for example, poses far less an existential threat to the overarching population of China, which has over 100 urban centers greater than million people, than to Israel, which has none. It is also notable that China’s population has significantly urbanized since it originally obtained nuclear weapons in 1964, illustrating that this factor is dynamic.57 A target’s vulnerability to a nuclear attack is an essential element of understanding nuclear threats, one that no previous quantitative analyses have accounted for. The second dimension to existential threats is the speed and effectiveness by which a nuclear-weapon state can deliver its nuclear weapons as part of an attack on the target. Ballistic missiles, in particular, emerged as a game-changing technology with respect to the delivery of nuclear weapons due to their speed and range and the difficulty of defending against them. Possessing ballistic-missile capabilities empowers states to project their nuclear capabilities far beyond their immediate region. Ballistic missiles enable states to launch nuclear strikes with the surprise and rapidity necessary for an effective decapitating first strike and are also central to most second-strike strategies. Indeed, the nuclear-arms race between the United States and Soviet Union was as much defined by the range, accuracy, and throw-weight of their ballistic missiles as it was by the size and potency of their nuclear warheads.58 The ability to deliver nuclear weapons is intrinsically tied to the threat they are capable of posing and the effects they have on state behavior.59 Finally, we view the perceived likelihood of use as a final, crucial element of existential threats. Nuclear weapons will be incredibly costly to use in any context, and their use against most states would be unimaginable for most leaders. As Brodie argues, the nuclear threats posed by other nuclear-weapon possessors are invariably a product of both their capabilities and their perceived intentions.60 Political factors that enhance the likelihood of governments actually using their nuclear weapons will enhance the latent existential threat that they pose to other states.61 For example, leaders will be more likely to use their nuclear weapons against entrenched adversaries than against nonrivals. These factors shape the threat perceptions of both the states posing the threats and those facing them.62 Significant disparities exist across nuclear-weapon states in terms of their capabilities in these areas and in how those capabilities translate into the specific existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to one another.63 We expect that how these factors align in determining the salience of nuclear-based existential threats will have a dramatic impact on how nuclear-armed states approach engaging in aggressive military actions toward one another. Employing this existential-threat measure as a continuum-based metric provides the means to assess how governments reference the independent capacity they have to threaten other states’ survival and the reciprocated threats they face. This follows from Jervis’s assertion that, “when the ultimate threat is the destruction of what each side values most and when inflicting damage on the opponent does not prevent him from doing likewise, then relative capability becomes less important than absolute.” 64 The existential threat a potential conflict initiator poses to a target is thus distinct from the threat the target poses back to the potential initiator. Separating the threats nuclear-armed states pose to and face from one another allows both factors to have distinct causal effects on states’ behaviors.65 As the basis for our subsequent theorizing, we argue that leaders of nuclear-armed states incorporate the existential threats they pose to potential targets and the reciprocal vulnerabilities they face from fellow nuclear-armed states in deciding whether to engage in aggressive military action against them. A rational actor might weigh these two issues in a similar fashion, where both sides move away from mutual suicide. As Schelling has discussed, though, policy makers practicing brinksmanship must often commit to engaging in irrational behaviors in order to achieve rationally derived outcomes.66 For decision makers dealing with nuclear threats or opportunities, “rationality in decisions therefore will appear to the policy-maker more as a goal to be sought—under the severe ‘cognitive constraints’ … than a premise to be assumed.” 67 This is compounded by the variation in the risk tolerance of various nuclear-weapon possessors, even if they all can be safely assumed to be rational actors. Any time policy makers are tolerant of non-zero risks that an undesired event may happen, there is a chance that it ultimately will occur.68 We therefore assume that, while survival is the core goal underlying state behavior, rational leaders can tolerate differing levels of risk when it comes to factoring how existential threats will shape their own behavior and the behavior of other states. On the basis of this logical core, we propose three distinct hypotheses that incorporate the potential role that nuclear-based existential threats could play in shaping states’ willingness to engage in military aggression. Deterrence-based hypothesis A deterrence-based hypothesis based on existential threats focuses on how nuclear threats faced by states affect their likelihood of initiating a militarized dispute with their nucleararmed adversary.69 The more salient the existential threat, the greater the potential of an aggressive use of force spiraling into a full-blown nuclear war. The strongest deterrent effects should be present when a potential conflict initiator has a high degree of confidence that the adversary possesses the ability to annihilate the majority of its major urban centers, making the potential costs of a full-scale nuclear conflict unthinkably high.70 This hypothesis presumes that policy makers are capable of discerning distinctions in the degree of existential threats posed by their nuclear-armed counterparts—factoring in knowledge about the approximate size of adversaries’ nuclear arsenals, their capacity to deliver those weapons, and information about their potential willingness to employ them. One complexity associated with this hypothesis is that states often keep secret at least some information about the nature of their capabilities. They may do so even if doing so undermines the deterrent value of their actual capabilities (e.g., the Soviet Union’s ill-conceived secret “Doomsday Machine” in Dr. Strangelove) or they may bluff about having greater capabilities than they actually do. Such private information may lead potential initiators to underestimate or overestimate the real extent of the existential threat they face from a target state.71 All else being equal, though, it could be expected that potential initiators will more likely be deterred from attacking adversaries posing existential threats than they will those they view as posing minor threats. HYPOTHESIS 1: The greater the existential threat that a nuclear-armed state faces from another nuclear-armed target state, the less likely it will be to initiate a MID with the target state. Emboldening hypothesis Our emboldening-based hypothesis focuses on the degree to which a potential initiator poses an existential threat to a target state. Such a threat should be considered conceptually distinct from the logic associated with nuclear superiority, although they share core elements. Nuclear superiority is premised on the idea that one side’s being able to threaten more destruction upon the other state gives it an advantage. In contrast, our emboldening hypothesis focuses on the sliding continuum of how much of an existential threat the leaders of a potential initiator think their state poses to a target—irrespective of the level of existential threat the target is capable of posing back. Being able to pose a greater existential threat to a target state might provide their leaders with more confidence about their ability to triumph in a dispute. They might think that a target state’s leaders will be more cautious about escalating disputes for fear of triggering an existentially threatening response. Being able to pose a salient existential threat to the target state could also be viewed as an insurance policy for potential initiators which they could rely on if the conflict were to take a disastrous turn. Even if overt nuclear threats are not made at any point during a state’s militarized aggression, the latent threat will still be looming in the background. A government’s ability to annihilate the population of a target state may not enhance its conventional capacities to win a localized border dispute, for example, but that capacity could very well embolden the government’s leaders to initiate the dispute when they otherwise would not have. The effects that the existential threat a potential initiator poses to a target do not suffer from the same informational challenges as the deterrence-based hypothesis, as potential initiators should have complete information concerning their own nuclear capabilities, their relationship with targets, and their targets’ major urban centers. HYPOTHESIS 2: The greater the existential threat that a nuclear armed-state poses to a nucleararmed target state, the more likely it will be to initiate a MID against it. Stability–instability paradox hypothesis Our approach’s emphasis on the specific existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to one another allows us to capture the nuances of the stability–instability paradox in ways that previous efforts to test the hypothesis could not. The stability–instability paradox is premised on the notion that two nuclear-armed states that are certain that all-out warfare would lead to their mutual annihilation have significant incentives to avoid escalating disputes to that level. In those cases, risk-acceptant states can engage in greater levels of military adventurism in terms of low-level conflicts because they have confidence that leaders on both sides—or at least one side—will prevent the conflict from escalating to fullscale war.72 The mere possession of nuclear weapons by both parties is not sufficient to convey to both parties that mutual annihilation is a highly probable outcome if a fullscale war should occur, however. For the deterrent mechanism to function within the hypothesis, both parties must be convinced that they share a common view that escalating to a nuclear war is unthinkable. Only when such stability has been introduced into nuclear dyads’ relationships, should we then expect to see the instability created in terms of a potential initiator’s enhanced willingness to engage in low-level military aggression. This suggests that the positive effects of the existential threat a potential initiator poses to a target are conditional upon the presence of a salient, reciprocated threat posed by the target to the potential initiator. The counterintuitive logic of this hypothesis suggests that minor conflicts between nuclear dyads should occur most frequently not when nuclear-armed states pose asymmetric or minimal existential threats to one another, but when both the potential initiator and the target pose serious existential threats to each other. This implies that the states facing the greatest risks of annihilating one another in a full-scale nuclear war will be the ones most likely to pick fights with one another. Past efforts at testing the stability– instability paradox in a large-n context could not capture the gradations in the existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to one another, which is essential to the theory’s logic.73 Employing a continuous measure of nuclear-based existential threats, however, can allow us to test the conditional nature of the relationship that posing such threats and facing such threats have on states’ conflict behavior. Thus, while our analysis does not comprehensively test the stability–instability paradox’s logic (because no nuclear wars have occurred between two nuclear-weapon possessors), we are able to evaluate one of its most controversial and testable implications. HYPOTHESIS 3: Conditional upon the presence of a salient existential threat from the target, the greater the existential threat that a nuclear-armed state poses to a target state, the more likely it will be to initiate a MID against it. Research design To test our hypotheses about the effects that nuclear existential threats have on states’ willingness to engage in international conflict, we conduct a quantitative analysis of the factors that affect nuclear-armed states’ likelihood of initiating MIDs with one another. Our units of analysis are the yearly observations of the potential-initiator–target directed dyads. Our data set of nuclear-armed directed dyads uses the dates that the United States, Soviet Union/Russia, the United Kingdom, France, China, Israel, South Africa, India, and Pakistan acquired their nuclear weapons and the sizes of their arsenals.74 In the next section, we detail how we operationalize our concept of nuclear-based existential threats and the rest of the variables we employ within our analysis. The initiation of MIDs We employ the initiation of MIDs by potential initiators against targets as our dependent variable, which allows us to evaluate the factors associated with why states engage in aggressive military behavior. MIDs constitute instances of conflict short of war in which states threaten, engage in displays of, or use military force against other states. We employ the directional initiation of MIDs as our dependent variable to operationalize our concept of military aggression.75 Our analysis does not specify the highest level of intensity to which a MID eventually escalates, because that aspect of a conflict is uncertain/unknowable at the time when a party decides to initiate an aggressive military act.76 We coded our dependent variable MID INITIATION to capture whether the potential initiator was the party responsibility for initiating a MID with the target in a given year.77 Employing directed dyads as our unit of analysis, the data set yields a total of 70 instances of MID initiation between nuclear-armed dyads between 1952 and 2001. We structure our dependent variable in an events-history format in which the threshold event being assessed is whether the potential initiator initiates a MID with the target in a given year. All at-risk dyads are included within our analysis. Observations in which no conflicts were initiated are coded as 0, while observations of conflict initiation are coded as 1. Coding nuclear existential threats and vulnerabilities To code the nuclear existential threat that the potential initiator poses to the target and the reciprocal existential vulnerability it faces from the target, we employ the NAT Index.78 The NAT Index captures the existential threat that a nuclear-armed state is capable of posing to another state as a product of the potential destruction it can inflict on a target’s population centers, its ability to strike the target with ballistic missiles, the presence of a rivalrous relationship, and the risks posed by the domestic instability of the threatening party. It captures variation in the extent of the existential threats that nuclear-armed states pose to one another, allowing distinct measures of the threats that potential initiators pose to targets and vice versa. As the NAT Index illustrates, most nuclear dyads do not involve symmetrical threats—suggesting that assumptions of symmetrical nuclear threats which have characterized most quantitative studies of the subject have been flawed. To operationalize the various concepts employed within the NAT Index, we adopt the following approaches. One of the key variables within the measure is the potential nuclear destruction that a nuclear-armed state’s arsenal poses to other states, depending on how the size of the state’s arsenal relates to the various numbers of strategically important population centers that other states possess. For each directed dyad, we first coded a count variable for the number of warheads in each state’s nuclear arsenal.79 We then collected data on the number of cities over a million people which states possessed from the “World Database of Large Urban Areas, 1950–2050.” 80 As large urban centers tend to represent the demographic, commercial, and cultural centers of their states, their destruction would pose the most salient threat to states’ continued political and economic viability. Targeting urban and industrial centers is a central component of countervalue nuclear targeting doctrines.81 The dataset lists the cities of over a million people which each state possessed at five-year increments. We aggregated these figures into a count variable for the total number of cities of over a million people a state possessed in a given year and then back- and forward-filled the gaps. For states lacking a city of over a million people (i.e., Israel), we counted their capital city as constituting a lone important urban center. To capture the specific potential nuclear destruction that dyad pairs posed to the each other, we adopted the assumption that it takes approximately four nuclear weapons to destroy a single urban area. Given the great variation in yield, reliability, and delivery platform which exists for nuclear warheads over time and across cases, we think four weapons per city constitutes a reasonable, generalizable threshold.82 Using this operative assumption, we code the variable POTENTIAL NUCLEAR DESTRUCTION as a three-point ordinal variable in which a 1 represents that a state’s nuclear arsenal can destroy less than 25 percent of its target’s large cities, a 2 represents that it can destroy between 25 and 75 percent of them, and a 3 represents the ability to destroy more than 75 percent of them.83 Within our directed dyads, separate indicators are thus coded for the potential nuclear destruction the potential initiator can inflict on the target and vice versa. The NAT Index also accounts for how the possession of ballistic missiles magnifies the scope of the threat that nuclear weapons pose, by providing a rapid, incredibly difficultto-defend-against means of delivering the weapons. The NAT Index gives extra weight to ballistic missiles over other types of delivery systems due to their combination of range, speed, and difficulty to intercept, which are presently unmatched by bombers or even cruise missiles.84 Indeed, nearly all states that acquire nuclear weapons seek to acquire long-range ballistic missiles as delivery platforms. To code the variable (BALLISTIC MISSILE CAPABILITY), we leverage data on the longest-range ballistic-missile system that a state possesses to obtain a maximum range the state can strike.85 We then compare this figure to the minimum distance figures separating the two states.86 If a dyad member cannot strike its counterpart with its ballistic missiles, we code this as 1. If it can, we code the variable as 2. We also code all the states that possess submarine-launched ballistic missiles as being able to strike all their dyadic counterparts. Within the context of how we construct the NAT Index in Equation 1, coding ballistic-missile capabilities this way allows our measure of existential threats to take into account how the speed, range, and difficulty associated with intercepting ballistic missiles magnify the threat of potential nuclear destruction.87 To account for the restraint that nuclear-armed states may employ in confronting nonrival versus rival states, we code a variable to denote the presence of a strategic rivalry between dyad pairs. We code the variable STRATEGIC RIVALRY using updated data on whether states possess rivalries over status, territory, or ideology, using the “Strategic Rivalry Dataset.” 88 We code the variable as 0.5 if no strategic rivalry is present and as 1 if such a rivalry is present. Lastly, the NAT Index takes into consideration how the presence of significant instability within a state’s governing authorities can potentially degrade the command and control over nuclear-weapon arsenals, enhancing the risks of unauthorized use or degraded decision making concerning their use. Destabilizing situations that pose substantial threat to states’ command and control over their nuclear arsenals are a relatively rare but substantively significant occurrence. We code STRATEGIC INSTABILITY to capture significant disruptions to the executive leadership within a state that would be responsible for managing and authorizing the use of nuclear weapons. This enhances the uncertainty surrounding the potential for a state to actually use its nuclear weapons when it might not otherwise do so, either intentionally or via unauthorized attack. To account for such events, we identify when coups, adverse regime changes, or national partitions have happened.89 Observation years in which these events took place and the following two years after are coded as 1, while they are coded as 0 for all the other years. Only a handful of these events have taken place within nuclear-armed states during the periods we analyze.90 Drawing on these constituent variables, we aggregate them to create the NAT Index using Equation 1. The multiplicative relationships between POTENTIAL NUCLEAR DESTRUCTION and BALLISTIC MISSILE CAPABILITY consider how possessing a ballisticmissile strike capability enhances the salience of the destructive nuclear threat states’ arsenals pose. STRATEGIC RIVALRY is also factored into this measure in order to capture the differential likelihood that nuclear weapons will be employed against rivals versus non-rivals. For example, the United States had pre-designated targets identified within
